sentences.text: 23 Svoboda Ulice.
S-V-O-B-O-D-A
U-L-period.
In Mukacevo.
And that was Kertvaros, which was the garden section of the town.
Kertvaros.
K-E-R-T-V-A-R-O-S.
And there's an umlaut there someplace.
We had a three bedrooms which were-- which was for one of them doubled for a dining room, too.
It had a-- a big dining room table, and it had a [NON-ENGLISH] here, and a [NON-ENGLISH] there.
And the kitchen had two tables in it.
One [NON-ENGLISH], one [NON-ENGLISH].
Then there was a veranda which was also a succah.
For the-- for the succah we opened up this ceiling, the attic.
And we put on it at all kinds of decorations for succah.
And then there a place in the summertime where we did the cooking and washing.
In winter there was the laundry washing.
And summertime summertime it was also as a kitchen to keep the upstairs cold.
And we had a pantry where all the food was put away-- all the-- the fruits and vegetables.
And under the veranda we had a sand cellar where all the root vegetables were stored for the whole winter-- cabbage, and parsley, and parsnips, and carrots, and potatoes, and whatever.
And then there was the cabbage, the barrel of cabbage which had-- which every one of these was a ritual.
To put the cabbage away was a party.
To flick the feathers was a party.
Everything was made into fun, because friends got together and, they were cleaning the cabbage.
And then my father was planing the cabbage, and I was put in the barrel to stomp on the cabbage.
And we were eating, and singing, and putting away food for the winter.
I was the youngest, and I was pampered.
And I was given-- I was given the-- the love that keeps me going.
And I'm giving back to my children today, because I got that.
My grandmother-- I come-- I one time I remember-- this is very interesting.
My sister was leaning against the-- then we didn't have the house yet.
We were living in [?
Bedinstein's ?] back yard, the [?
Bedinstein's ?] courtyard.
It was a small, little place.
And my sister was leaning against the credenza in the kitchen, and crying.
And my other sister was I remember, get Helen.
And-- and she came home, and she was crying.
And I saw a-- a cherubic covered with lots of white around her.
And a men in a black suit, sort of rubbing his ties and leaning back and forth, who was the doctor.
And my grandmother who had this little round chubby face was dying.
And that's how I remember that face because I saw it once.
And from the-- my conscious memory of her.
I may have seen her, of course, many times.
But that was the vivid memory I have of her.
Her name was Ruchal.
That I can't tell you.
Ruchal Greenfeld.
I don't want to give a date.
It could have been 1931 or 1932.
I cannot recall.
I would not be able to make it, exactly.
No.
My grandfather was an [NON-ENGLISH] doing business.
And he carried all his bank books with himself.
He was a businessman who felt his pockets was his-- were his office.
And he collapsed in [NON-ENGLISH] and died.
And somebody removed his books from his.
Because in Europe, you have a book, you have your money.
And then I remember my mother was telling me about how she went about to go to banks to get that money.
And that was not very easy.
But she got.
She got the money.
Whether it was-- there was more to be had, I don't know.
But he was very, very comfortable.
And he had a very good business mind.
And he knew how to-- how to put the money together.
And my grandmother was able to get anything from him she wanted.
Because she traveled to Karlsbad all the time and she just enjoyed life.
Because I know from the dishes we had.
This come from here, this came from there, and all the nice things.
So she must have known how to get my grandfather to part with his money.
Yes.
Konigsbach, my father's mother, was married to a Konigsbach.
And he died.
And she was married to another person who did not pull his weight, according to her, and she divorced him.
In those days-- I must give her a lot of credit.
And then she married a Schonberger who had a very, very illustrious family.
And that-- that really is another story by itself.
But she married a Schonberger.
From that union they had Adolf.
And my uncle Konig--
Uncle Adolf.
Yes.
Yes.
And he was Schonberger.
But my uncle Isidore was Konigsbach.
And then he came to this country.
My uncle Konigsbach said, I don't want my brother to have a different name.
I'm going to change from Konigsbach to Schonberger.
And when we came, he says, forgive me girls.
I'm not going to change my name back to Konigsbach, because I don't want to get a judge more confused.
Because the judge asked me, why do you want to change from Konigsbach to Schonberger.
So he says, I'll stay now Schonberger.
I went to the [NON-ENGLISH], to the-- to the kindergarten.
And then I went to the elementary school.
And then to the Hungarian [NON-ENGLISH].
And so many of my friends went to the gymnasium.
But my friends were Hungarian, and then Hungarian speaking.
We spoke Hungarian with my friends, Czechish.
And so I went to the [NON-ENGLISH].
Well, I had no problem with-- with any anti-Semitism there.
I had my friends, and I had my-- my associations with them after school.
And I didn't have any problem at that time.
I knew that they have no use for us.
That I knew.
Their distance.
But around the neighborhood that's where their-- their behavior was very bad.
They would Sunday, after the church, we knew that we have to stay off the streets.
And if we had to go someplace, we had to be very careful.
Because after the Church, they were so incited with those sermons the priests gave, they were throwing stones at the Jews-- especially the children.
They-- they were fierce.
And they were-- they were telling us that, you were a Jew, and you are a Christ-killer.
So throughout my childhood, I have always had that feeling that the Jews are hated.
And they behaved very, very badly, those children.
And it all came from the home.
The school-- that was interesting because, we had the children from different areas.
It wasn't in the same.
My neighborhood-- most of them went to Hebrew schools, Hebrew school [NON-ENGLISH].
And the children in school, they maybe they were just holding back a little.
Because we knew the-- the undertone.
We felt it.
But we didn't mix with them that much.
So and it was more of a civilized behavior.
But when you came to the-- to the playing, and when they cornered someone they could beat him up, they did it.
And that was under the Czechs yet.
And then when the Hungarians were there, they were just-- they let loose of their anger.
My father, as I told you, he was-- he thought that the-- the Hungary was his home, and he loved that.
Because he grew, he was born in Hungary.
Yes.
And we had in our-- we had in our wood shed we had [NON-ENGLISH].
A painting of [NON-ENGLISH]-- an oil painting with a lovely frame.
It was hidden in the wood shed wrapped up in blankets.
All that time it was there.
And then he took that out, polished it up, put it into the window in the store with the-- with the flags around it.
And draped-- they called them [NON-ENGLISH], which is a draping of a flag.
And that evening, he went out to greet the Hungarian soldiers who are coming, who have occupied us.
And he was standing there.
And he saw that this man, this soldier beat up a Jew-- a bearded Jew who was going to greet him also.
And he was so-- he was so disappointed.
He could not believe that these people whom he waited for have been-- are so bad.
Before-- the first of all, they were not good.
They-- I mean the history shows that [NON-ENGLISH], and his henchmen, and all, they were all bad.
But he had a-- he had a good memory from about them.
But he went home that evening, and he died that night.
December 28th, 1939 he died.
Heartache.
When he came home, he was so unhappy, so outraged.
He was telling what happened on the [NON-ENGLISH] what happened.
And it just-- it just made him so very, very ill.
And that he died.
Well, he was smoking, but most everybody was smoking.
Well, you can't-- you can't say that this is the one incident.
But it led up to his smoking.
And his war experiences, it must have led up to it.
But he was lean and he was smoking.
So that is my memory of his-- when he died.
Well, and then the Hungarian started their pressure.
They restricted the food.
They gave rations.
The Jews got less.
They were-- you had to prove that you were Hungarian background.
And if you couldn't prove it, they deported people to Poland.
And my mother had to find all the papers that to prove it, that we are not Polish.
Because if you cannot prove it, you are Polish.
And that going to these little offices, to these notaries, and wherever they kept the documents.
It-- it was such a difficult thing to get, because they were not cooperating.
And all the bribes, and lying, and cheating they did not to give you those papers.
The pressure was put on.
Well we did get the papers and we were not deported.
And then there were the to check if you are doing something illegal, they would just go to a door, knock on it, and come in and beat up anybody they felt like.
And looked, ransacked the house to check for something that is not correct.
Which was only anything.
They used anything to intimidate the people.
And--
They did not come to our house, no.
But we heard it-- we heard it.
We heard these happenings in the morning regularly.
And then there was this place that they took people in to the [NON-ENGLISH].
And they were beating them, and hurting them.
And it was just a very, very intimidating and a hard-- a hard way to imagine that this can be done by people who you would have thought were your friends.
And then they were looking to license the businesses.
You had to reapply for a license.
My mother was now a widow, and she had to get the business on a widow's permit.
And that was a difficult.
And every time you went into these official places, as soon as they saw a Jew, they immediately gave you a very, very tough time.
And you felt every step of the way that they are out to hurt you.
And then the rationing of the sugar.
Rationing of-- of butter.
Rationing of everything.
And you had to somehow find a way.
Well we had stashed away flour.
And we stashed away the larder with the goose fat, chicken fat.
We had food.
And we shared our food with another family who had 11 children.
And we had to-- we had to help somebody because everybody had to have someone that they should help out so wouldn't die of hunger.
These people did not plan ahead with 11 children and always-- they were very poor.
They had a big house that they built.
They put everything into the house.
And they were very poor after that.
And I would take my suit briefcase and put potatoes into it and whatever my mother put in there for them.
And I would go there to look like I'm going there to study, and gave them food.
I was older by then.
And I would-- I would take it there.
And they would cook the potatoes and make it for right away.
You could see that they were hungry.
And then you couldn't get out, because they would arrest you for whatever.
Some-- you needed papers.
And they would harass you.
And they would-- it was just a constant harassment with-- with beatings.
And taking them off to this horrible place to-- to strip them, and beat them, and search them, and question them.
And then they would send people to take the money.
If you're going to pay so much and so much, you can buy freedom.
And then we would have to get-- we would have to give them some money.
And nobody wanted to give everything, because there was no more to be had.
And the-- the Jews came in.
The people from the temples came in and said, look, we have to get so much and so much or else they're going to take away a few families or whoever they can just to show their power.
Were these Jewish officials?
The Jewish officials who came around.
Because they went to these Jewish officials and said, now we want so much and so much from you.
So you better get it, or this and this is going to happen.
So they came around, and they tried to get it.
And they did most of the time.
They got it.
Which made again for great insecurity and unhappiness.
And they were squeezing and squeezing.
And then they-- the children were taken to labor camps.
It was the heartbreak, and the-- the fear, and the death.
They killed.
And they were just putting you through hell every single day and night.
How much of this did you actually see yourself?
I have-- I have not been-- I have seen beatings.
I have seen beatings.
But I was-- I was-- I only overheard while they were talking the next day.
You can't help it, because children will hear through thick walls.
And the fear was there.
Constant fear.
And there were those [? chanters. ?] They were the peacekeepers.
They had those rooster feathers on their helmet.
And when they came around, you could see them from the window that their feather was going.
And then you knew that they're out for trouble.
They're going to do something destructive.
And they were very, very poorly behaved.
They were very bad peasants.
They were given a gun, and authority, and go in to see what harm you can do to the Jews.
And these peasants were very low on the human-- human chain.
They just went wild.
And this went on for years.
And then they would deport people.
And then you would see the people taken off and beaten on the way.
They just had-- they just had this-- let's punish, let's hurt, let's kill, let's loot because they are Jews.
And that constant fear, that constant pain, and that constant worry.
And a mother to worry about her children which is so much-- so much harder for a mother.
And a child is-- if you tell a child, don't worry.
It will be OK.
A child will relax.
A child will say, well OK.
Mother knows best.
And if she says it, that's what it's going to be.
But it never happened.
And then it was just-- it was just a time that you could not see the end of it.
Then came in 1944 they took-- they had the ghetto.
Well, first of all I was-- I went to Czech school.
The first year I went to Hungarian, and then switched over to Czech.
And then when the Hungarians came, I changed back to Hungarian school.
But there was no problem, because I spoke the language-- both languages fluently.
And--
I am--
Well I was born in '29.
And the schools-- already the teachers, the Hungarian teachers have shown their anti-Semitism.
They did-- they did show that the Jewish culture is not to be admired.
Well, then-- first let me go back to when I was in first grade, which was in the Hungarian school.
This teacher said-- that was under the Czechs.
And the Czechs were good, but they had people.
There is always everywhere, there is anti-Semitism everywhere.
But the government itself was-- was not.
And this teacher tells the class that the Jewish language is for-- for-- I don't know how he expressed himself.
Not for civilized people.
It isn't a nice language.
And anyone who speaks it is not civilized.
And I took that very seriously.
And I went home, and I said, I never want to speak Yiddish again.
And my mother smiled and said, well that will change.
And I had this feeling all through my growing up years that Yiddish is not a nice language.
And I refused to speak it.
But I understand everything, of course.
But I don't.
I don't use the Yiddish for-- I don't make conversation in Yiddish, because I can't.
But I know all the words that come up.
And I make all kinds of references in Yiddish.
There was the favoritism that they have shown for the Christian children.
And the-- the Jewish children were-- they were very good.
They did their work, and they knew their-- And in class, I can't say that in class that they didn't call on the Jewish children.
The praise wasn't there for Jewish children.
They did not say, well, I'll help you with that.
But they did not come out and openly show their anti-Semitism.
And the higher the class, the less-- the more subdued they were in showing their feelings.
But it was there.
You could-- you would always know your place.
But out-- out in the street and in business.
And when they-- I remember my-- we had this business.
And we had to collect.
We had to buy tax stamps.
In other words, you bought the tax stamps in advance to do your business.
And these Hungarians would come in, and they would make derogatory remarks about that.
We are making more money and we are taking advantage of the Christians.
And then he says, I want to see your books because you could only charge so much.
There was 33% of profit you could charge.
And he says, I want to see your books if you really charge that much.
Show me your billing.
And the intimidation.
And sometimes you had to charge a little more, because it wasn't enough.
And then, when this happened, then you went back to just charge what they allowed.
And the intimidation was there.
Whoever-- whatever and whoever could would hurt.
In school that the patriotism was very-- they were constantly drilling you on patriotism.
No.
The Czech people have now gone back to Czechoslovakia, to Czech, to wherever they came from before they have come to Carpathian section.
They now went back.
And all the teachers now were Hungarian.
But of course those who went to the Hebrew school, they had their Hebrew teachers.
Only we, who have gone to the Hungarian school, have-- have had new teachers.
They came from other parts.
It was the patriotism about what Hungary is, and how we have to love and respect.
And the history had to be very carefully learned, because then you are not-- you are not patriotic.
And the songs, and going to see the movies, the propaganda movies.
And they were just trying to show that there is no one as good as the Hungarians.
Well at 1940-- end of 1943-- it wasn't '43 already.
I had to stop, and get out of school, and go to hide with my family.
We went to-- to the country.
But that didn't last long, because we couldn't go to school.
They were just-- we didn't dare.
It was-- they were catching people and deporting them.
But they were doing their attrition all along.
So we didn't-- I didn't go to school.
A lot of people fell out.
And we went to--
Yeah, 14.
Yeah.
And then we went to the country where my sister lived.
But there they said, that's not a good idea, because all these peasants know you're here.
And they going to come, and they're going to tell the authorities, and you're going to be taken away faster.
So we went back to Mukacevo.
And then the ghetto was formed.
And then--
That's another episode in our life.
There were two ghettos.
One was by the-- by the-- marketplace.
There was a market area.
That part was one ghetto.
And then the other part was at the [?
Yiddish ?] [NON-ENGLISH] on the other part of the town.
And they-- they came and chased us out.
And they said, you have--
Out of our homes to go to the ghetto.
Take what you want and go.
That was-- that was around Passover.
And we were told to get our belongings.
And we would be assigned.
We had friends living in a-- in a very nice house.
And they gave us a room there-- for all of us, one room.
And we packed our warm clothes, and whatever we could, and went there.
And we stayed in the ghetto for one month.
And again there they would take people--
That was the-- the marketplace.
And we were-- we took some stuff.
And we were allowed only once to go back to the house.
And it was that time when I really turned to the house for one more-- one more look.
I will never forget that.
We went back, and the house looked so beautiful, and so big, and so-- it wasn't that big, but in my mind it was so big.
And the flower garden, which was nothing was done.
And everything there was just the grass whatever was grown.
And it was just not-- it was a memory I cannot get out of my mind.
That was the last time I said I thought I'll see that.
Well, I went back in 1968 and I saw it.
And I have some pictures of it which is dilapidated.
Because we had vines growing over the veranda, and we had the greenery everywhere.
And that came in.
And then when I went back in '68, there was nothing growing.
No grapevines growing.
